name: saga-orchestration
description: Implement saga patterns for distributed transactions and cross-aggregate workflows. Use when coordinating multi-step business processes, handling compensating transactions, or managing long-running workflows.
capabilities: Implement orchestration-based saga patterns with centralized coordinators
Implement choreography-based saga patterns using event-driven architecture
Design and execute compensating transactions for failure recovery
Handle step timeouts and failure escalation in long-running workflows
Create idempotent saga steps for safe retry handling
Build order fulfillment and approval workflows across services
author: sickn33
securitySummary: All 19 static findings are false positives. The pattern-matching scanner misidentified Python code templates as Ruby shell execution, markdown text as cryptographic algorithms, and service names in diagrams as C2 indicators. The skill contains legitimate saga orchestration patterns for distributed systems with no security risks.
valueStatement: Coordinate multi-step business processes across microservices with reliable compensation handling when failures occur.
userTitle: Implement Saga Patterns for Distributed Transactions
limitations: Does not provide runtime execution environment or message infrastructure
Does not include database or persistence layer implementations
Requires integration with existing event publishing systems
Does not handle distributed saga coordination across process boundaries

useCases.title: E-commerce Order Processing
useCases.description: Implement multi-step order fulfillment with inventory reservation, payment processing, and shipping orchestration with automatic rollback on failures.

useCases.title: Financial Transaction workflows
useCases.description: Create reliable financial processes with atomic compensation for multi-step transfers, refunds, and reconciliation tasks.

useCases.title: Cross-Service Approval Systems
useCases.description: Build approval workflows that coordinate across multiple services with automatic compensation if approvals are revoked or expire.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Saga Structure
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a Python saga orchestrator base class that manages saga state (Started, Pending, Compensating, Completed, Failed) with methods for starting a saga, handling step completion, and executing compensation in reverse order on failure.
promptTemplates.scenario: Need a foundation for implementing saga patterns in a Python microservices application

promptTemplates.title: Order Fulfillment Workflow
promptTemplates.prompt: Implement an OrderFulfillmentSaga that coordinates reserve_inventory, process_payment, create_shipment, and send_confirmation steps with appropriate compensation actions for each step.
promptTemplates.scenario: Building a complete order processing pipeline with rollback capability

promptTemplates.title: Choreography-Based Events
promptTemplates.prompt: Create an event-driven choreography saga that uses an event bus to publish and subscribe to events like OrderCreated, InventoryReserved, PaymentProcessed, and ShipmentCreated without a central orchestrator.
promptTemplates.scenario: Implementing decentralized saga coordination using event-driven architecture

promptTemplates.title: Saga with Timeout Handling
promptTemplates.prompt: Extend the saga orchestrator to include timeout handling for each step. Add scheduled timeout checks that trigger compensation if a step does not complete within the configured time window.
promptTemplates.scenario: Adding reliability to long-running saga steps that may hang or fail silently

bestPractices: Always design compensation actions before implementing forward actions - compensation must be reliable
Use idempotency keys for all commands to safely handle duplicate messages without side effects
Store correlation IDs across all events and logs to trace saga execution across service boundaries
antiPatterns: Do not assume saga steps complete instantly - always implement async handling and state persistence
Do not skip compensation testing - simulate failure at every step and verify rollback works
Do not couple services directly - use async event messaging to maintain loose coupling

faq.question: What is the difference between orchestration and choreography saga patterns?
faq.answer: Orchestration uses a central coordinator that directs all steps. Choreography uses events where each service reacts to events from other services. Orchestration provides better visibility and control; choreography offers lower coupling.

faq.question: How do compensations work in saga patterns?
faq.answer: Compensations are undo actions that reverse the effects of completed steps. They run in reverse order when a step fails. For example, if payment succeeds but shipping fails, the compensation would refund the payment.

faq.question: What happens if a compensation also fails?
faq.answer: The saga enters a Failed state after compensation completes. Operators should be notified for manual intervention. Design compensations to be idempotent and reliable to minimize this scenario.

faq.question: How do timeouts work in saga patterns?
faq.answer: Timeouts are tracked per step. If a step does not complete within the timeout window, the saga treats it as a failure and triggers compensation. Use a scheduler to check for timed-out steps.

faq.question: Can saga patterns handle duplicate messages?
faq.answer: Yes, by using idempotency keys. Each command includes a unique key, and handlers check if the key was already processed before executing the action. This prevents double-charging or double-shipping.

faq.question: When should I not use saga patterns?
faq.answer: Avoid sagas when you need strong consistency (ACID transactions), when operations are very short and can use local transactions, or when the business process is simple enough for a single service.
